request.question: "Rags to Riches" is a 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross, the best-known version of the song, recorded by Tony Bennett with which Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor, known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards?

title: Everybody Loves a Lover
Dance with Me (Debelah Morgan song)
I'm Not at All in Love
Jerry Ross (composer)
Rags to Riches (song)
Steam Heat
Hernando's Hideaway
John Murray Anderson's Almanac
Whatever Lola Wants
Percy Faith
sentences: "Everybody Loves a Lover" is a popular song which was a hit single for Doris Day in 1958.
Its lyricist, Richard Adler, and its composer, Robert Allen, were both best known for collaborations with other partners.
The music Allen composed, aside from this song, was usually for collaborations with Al Stillman, and Adler wrote the lyrics after the 1955 death of his usual composing partner, Jerry Ross.
"Dance with Me" is a song by American R&B singer Debelah Morgan, composed by Morgan and music producer Giloh Morgan.
Released in the year 2000, it was the first single produced from Morgan's third studio album of the same name.
Its protagonist declares that she only wants to dance with her lover that night.
"Dance with Me" peaked at <nowiki>#</nowiki>8 on the "Billboard" Singles Chart on December 26, 2000 (after reaching the Top 40 on October 3), and has been added to many hit compilation albums.
"Dance with Me" received international acclaim and has been performed in different versions, including a special Walt Disney version and a Spanish version "Baila Conmigo."
Morgan's "Dance With Me" was based on "Hernando's Hideaway" written by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross, who are given credit on the song.
"I'm Not at All in Love" is a popular song written by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross, published in 1954.
It was first presented in the musical "The Pajama Game" by Janis Paige.

Jerry Ross (born Jerold Rosenberg; March 9, 1926 – November 11, 1955) was an American lyricist and composer whose works with Richard Adler for the musical theater include "The Pajama Game" and "Damn Yankees", winners of Tony Awards in 1955 and 1956, respectively, in both the "Best Musical" and "Best Composer and Lyricist" categories.
"Rags to Riches" is a 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross.
The best-known version of the song, recorded by Tony Bennett with Percy Faith and his orchestra, was number one for eight weeks on the Billboard chart in 1953 and became a gold record.

In the same year, a version by David Whitfield reached number three in the British charts and one by Billy Ward and his Dominoes with Jackie Wilson singing lead made number two on Billboard's R&B chart.
Later recordings by Sunny & the Sunliners (#45 in 1963) and Elvis Presley (#33 in 1971) also made the Billboard charts.
"Steam Heat" is a show tune from the 1954 Broadway musical "The Pajama Game", written by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross.
"Hernando's Hideaway" is a tango show tune, largely in long metre, from the musical "The Pajama Game", written by Jerry Ross and Richard Adler and published in 1954.
It was sung in the stage and film versions of the musical by Carol Haney.
John Murray Anderson's Almanac is a musical revue, featuring the music of the songwriting team of Richard Adler and Jerry Ross, as well as other composers.
It was conceived by John Murray Anderson.
"Whatever Lola Wants" is a popular song, sometimes rendered as "Whatever Lola Wants, Lola Gets".
The music and words were written by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross for the 1955 musical play "Damn Yankees".
The song is sung by Lola, the Devil's assistant, a part originated by Gwen Verdon, who reprised the role in the film.
The saying was inspired by Lola Montez, an Irish-born "Spanish dancer" and mistress of King Ludwig I of Bavaria, who later became a San Francisco Gold Rush vamp.
Percy Faith (April 7, 1908 – February 9, 1976) was a Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor, known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards.
He is often credited with popularizing the "easy listening" or "mood music" format.
Faith became a staple of American popular music in the 1950s and continued well into the 1960s.

Though his professional orchestra-leading career began at the height of the swing era, Faith refined and rethought orchestration techniques, including use of large string sections, to soften and fill out the brass-dominated popular music of the 1940s.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: "Rags to Riches" is a 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross, the best-known version of the song, recorded by Tony Bennett with which Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor, known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards?
hop1_docs: Rags to Riches (song) | "Rags to Riches" is a 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross.  The best-known version of the song, recorded by Tony Bennett with Percy Faith and his orchestra, was number one for eight weeks on the Billboard chart in 1953 and became a gold record.  In the same year, a version by David Whitfield reached number three in the British charts and one by Billy Ward and his Dominoes with Jackie Wilson singing lead made number two on Billboard's R&B chart.  Later recordings by Sunny & the Sunliners (#45 in 1963) and Elvis Presley (#33 in 1971) also made the Billboard charts.

Percy Faith | Percy Faith (April 7, 1908 – February 9, 1976) was a Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor, known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards.  He is often credited with popularizing the "easy listening" or "mood music" format.  Faith became a staple of American popular music in the 1950s and continued well into the 1960s.  Though his professional orchestra-leading career began at the height of the swing era, Faith refined and rethought orchestration techniques, including use of large string sections, to soften and fill out the brass-dominated popular music of the 1940s.

Everybody Loves a Lover | "Everybody Loves a Lover" is a popular song which was a hit single for Doris Day in 1958.  Its lyricist, Richard Adler, and its composer, Robert Allen, were both best known for collaborations with other partners.  The music Allen composed, aside from this song, was usually for collaborations with Al Stillman, and Adler wrote the lyrics after the 1955 death of his usual composing partner, Jerry Ross.

Sleigh Ride | "Sleigh Ride" is a popular light orchestral Christmas music standard composed by Leroy Anderson.  The composer had the original idea for the piece during a heat wave in July 1946 and finished the work in February 1948.  It was originally an instrumental piece; the lyrics, about someone asking another to join them for a ride in a sleigh, were written by Mitchell Parish in 1950.  The orchestral version was first recorded in 1949 by Arthur Fiedler and the Boston Pops Orchestra.  Sleigh Ride was a hit record on RCA Victor Red Seal 49-0515 (45 rpm) / 10-1484 (78 rpm), and has become a signature song for the orchestra.  The 45 rpm version was originally issued on red vinyl.  The Pops has also recorded the song with John Williams, their conductor from 1979 to 1995, and Keith Lockhart, their current conductor.

Robert Russell Bennett | Robert Russell Bennett (June 15, 1894 – August 18, 1981) was an American composer and arranger, best known for his orchestration of many well-known Broadway and Hollywood musicals by other composers such as Irving Berlin, George Gershwin, Jerome Kern, Cole Porter, and Richard Rodgers.  In 1957 and 2008, Bennett received Tony Awards recognizing his orchestrations for Broadway shows.  Early in his career he was often billed as Russell Bennett.

Norrie Paramor | Norman William "Norrie" Paramor (15 May 1914 – 9 September 1979) was a British record producer, composer, arranger, pianist, bandleader, and orchestral conductor.  He is best known for his work with Cliff Richard and the Shadows, both together and separately, steering their early careers and producing and arranging most of their material from the late 1950s to the early 1970s.  Paramor was a composer of studio albums, theatrical productions, and film scores.

My Heart Tells Me | "My Heart Tells Me" is a song written by Harry Warren with lyrics by Mack Gordon.  It is the theme to the 1943 American musical film "Sweet Rosie O'Grady", in which it is sung by lead actress Betty Grable.  A 1940s standard, the song has been recorded by numerous artists, including Frank Sinatra, Tony Bennett, Nat King Cole, and Etta Jones.  The film's popularity contributed to the commercial success of the version of the song recorded by bandleader Glen Gray and his Casa Loma Orchestra with vocals by singer Eugenie Baird; titled "My Heart Tells Me (Should I Believe My Heart?)" , it topped "The Billboard"<nowiki>'s</nowiki> National Best Selling Retail Records chart for five weeks in 1944.
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: "Rags to Riches" (1953 song); Percy Faith.

- "Rags to Riches" — 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross.  
- "'recorded by Tony Bennett with Percy Faith and his orchestra'." (passage [1])  
- Percy Faith: "April 7, 1908 – February 9, 1976"; described as "Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor" (passage [2]).  
- Percy Faith is "known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards." (passage [2])  
- Passage [1] also states the Bennett/Faith version was "number one for eight weeks on the Billboard chart in 1953."

Ambiguity / missing facts: none — the passages unambiguously identify the bandleader; no additional bridging entity required.

Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
1. No additional retrieval needed (passages already identify the bandleader).  
2. (Optional verification) Fetch "Rags to Riches (song)" — lede/infobox and "Percy Faith" — lede/infobox and "Career" sections for confirmation.
response.hop2_query: Percy Faith lede infobox career Rags to Riches
hop2_docs: Rags to Riches (song) | "Rags to Riches" is a 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross.  The best-known version of the song, recorded by Tony Bennett with Percy Faith and his orchestra, was number one for eight weeks on the Billboard chart in 1953 and became a gold record.  In the same year, a version by David Whitfield reached number three in the British charts and one by Billy Ward and his Dominoes with Jackie Wilson singing lead made number two on Billboard's R&B chart.  Later recordings by Sunny & the Sunliners (#45 in 1963) and Elvis Presley (#33 in 1971) also made the Billboard charts.

Percy Faith | Percy Faith (April 7, 1908 – February 9, 1976) was a Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor, known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards.  He is often credited with popularizing the "easy listening" or "mood music" format.  Faith became a staple of American popular music in the 1950s and continued well into the 1960s.  Though his professional orchestra-leading career began at the height of the swing era, Faith refined and rethought orchestration techniques, including use of large string sections, to soften and fill out the brass-dominated popular music of the 1940s.
Rags to riches (disambiguation) | Rags to riches refers to a rise from poverty to wealth.
From Riches to Rags | From Riches to Rags is a 1980 Hong Kong film written and directed by John Woo starring Ricky Hui and Johnny Koo.
Rags to Riches (horse) | Rags to Riches (foaled February 27, 2004, in Kentucky) is an American thoroughbred racehorse who in 2007 became the first filly to win the Belmont Stakes in over a century.
Rags to Riches (video game) | Rags to Riches is a side-scrolling action-adventure game released in 1985 for the Commodore 64, developed by Bob Keener and published by Melody Hall Publishing Corp.

Twelfth Street Rag | "Twelfth Street Rag" is a ragtime musical composition published by Euday L. Bowman in 1914.  Years earlier, a friend of Bowman's known only as "Raggedy Ed" declared his intention to open a pawn shop on 12th Street while the two were walking along it.  Bowman is rumored to have said "If you get rich on those three balls, I'll write a piece on three notes to make myself rich."  The result was "The 12th Street Rag," one of the most famous and best-selling rags of the ragtime era.
response.summary_2: The best-known version was recorded by Tony Bennett with Percy Faith and his orchestra. Percy Faith was a Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards.
